1: ἀλήθειαν λέγω ἐν Χριστῷ — three layers of attestation frame this claim: "I am telling the truth," "in Christ" (Paul's union with Christ as the ground of truthfulness), and "my conscience bearing witness in the Holy Spirit." The triple reinforcement signals Paul is anticipating incredulity; the emotional claim of vv. 2–3 would strain belief.
2: λύπη (grief) and ἀδιάλειπτος ὀδύνη (unceasing anguish) — two distinct terms. Odynē is the sharper word, used for the physical agony of wounds (cf. Luke 16:24–25); adialeipton (unceasing) marks it as chronic, not episodic. The accumulation establishes, before the difficult theological argument of chapter 9, that Paul's treatment of Israel's situation is not cool academic detachment.
3: ηὐχόμην — imperfect of eύchomαi, not aorist; the imperfect marks the wish as ongoing or as one recognizably impossible even as expressed: "I was wishing / I could wish." The wish to become ἀνάθεμα (accursed, under the ban, cut off from Christ) for the sake of kinspeople mirrors Moses in Exod 32:32 and demonstrates that Paul's theological argument does not produce indifference to ethnic Israel.
4: The catalog of Israel's privileges proceeds as predicate nominatives: υἱοθεσία (adoption/sonship), δόξα (the divine glory-presence), αἱ διαθῆκαι (the covenants — plural, acknowledging multiple covenant-making moments: Abraham, Sinai, David), ἡ νομοθεσία (the law-giving), ἡ λατρεία (the temple worship/service), αἱ ἐπαγγελίαι (the promises).
5: τὸ κατὰ σάρκα (according to the flesh) — the earthly genealogical descent of the Messiah within Israel. The clause that follows — ὁ ὢν ἐπὶ πάντων θεὸς εὐλογητὸς εἰς τοὺς αἰῶνας (who is over all, God blessed forever) — is genuinely contested: is it an appositional description of Christ (an early NT confession of his deity) or a separate doxology to God the Father? Syntactically the natural reading links the clause to Christ as subject, but manuscript punctuation is uncertain. Both readings have sustained patristic and modern defenders.
6: οὐχ οἷον ὅτι ἐκπέπτωκεν (it is not as though [God's word] has fallen/failed) — the perfect tense ekpeptōken is important: the word of God has not yet fallen and remains standing. The assertion οὐ γὰρ πάντες οἱ ἐξ Ἰσραὴλ οὗτοι Ἰσραήλ distinguishes the ethnic community ("those from Israel") from the covenant community ("Israel"): physical descent does not constitute membership in the entity God was promising to.
7: ἐν Ἰσαὰκ κληθήσεταί σοι σπέρμα (in Isaac your seed shall be called) — the future passive klēthēsetai: the divine calling determines which line counts as the promised seed, not biological derivation. The quotation (Gen 21:12) shifts the burden of identification from genealogy to election by naming.
8: τέκνα τοῦ θεοῦ = τέκνα τῆς ἐπαγγελίας — the equation is exact: children of God and children of promise are coextensive. Physical descent produces physical children; the promise of God produces children of God. The logic runs through the Abraham argument of chapter 4.
9: κατὰ τὸν καιρὸν τοῦτον (at this appointed time) — kairos, not chronos: an appointed, significant time rather than mere clock-time. The Gen 18:10 promise depicts a God who operates according to a schedule — the promise of Isaac is a divinely-timed intervention, not a natural event.
10: The syntax emphasizes common origin: ἐξ ἑνός (from one man, Isaac). Unlike the Isaac/Ishmael case which could be attributed to different mothers, Esau and Jacob share both parents and the same conception. This eliminates maternal lineage and birth order as variables, leaving divine purpose as the only differentiating factor.
11: ἵνα ἡ κατ' ἐκλογὴν πρόθεσις τοῦ θεοῦ μένῃ — "so that God's purpose according to election might stand." Three terms compound: eklogē (election/selection), prothesis (deliberate prior purpose), and menē (subjunctive of μένω, to remain/stand). The purpose clause is explicit: the timing of the announcement (before birth, before works) is designed to establish that standing is contingent on God's prior purpose, not on human action.
12: ὁ μείζων δουλεύσει τῷ ἐλάσσονι (the greater will serve the lesser) — the oracle reverses expected primogeniture; in the ANE world, the firstborn held the dominant social position. The passive ἐρρέθη (it was said) identifies the reversal as divinely spoken, not incidentally worked out.
13: τὸν Ἰακὼβ ἠγάπησα, τὸν δὲ Ἠσαῦ ἐμίσησα (Jacob I loved, Esau I hated) — from Mal 1:2–3. The Hebrew sānēʾ (hate) in Semitic idiom can function as a contrast meaning "love less" or "disregard" (cf. Luke 14:26); in Malachi it refers to the nations descended from Esau, not Esau's individual fate. Paul uses it not to address personal salvation but to illustrate that God's elective purpose operates without prior claim from the human side.
14: μὴ ἀδικία παρὰ τῷ θεῷ (is there unrighteousness with God?) — the negative particle mē expects a "no" answer. Paul formulates the objection directly (as in 3:5), then refuses to let the logic of election collapse into a charge of divine injustice. Mē genoito (by no means) is his strongest negative rhetorical marker.
15: The Exod 33:19 quotation — ἐλεήσω ὃν ἂν ἐλεῶ, καὶ οἰκτιρήσω ὃν ἂν οἰκτίρω (I will have mercy on whom I have mercy, and I will have compassion on whom I have compassion) — is given to Moses at the moment Israel had broken the covenant with the golden calf. Mercy is exercised toward a people who had just forfeited any claim to it; the pattern of pure mercy is established at Israel's lowest covenant-keeping point.
16: ἄρα οὖν οὐ τοῦ θέλοντος οὐδὲ τοῦ τρέχοντος (not of the one who wills nor of the one who runs) — two present participles used as nouns representing human effort in its two modes: willing (internal resolve) and running (active exertion). The double negative is emphatic: neither contributes to the outcome. The single operative agent is τοῦ ἐλεῶντος θεοῦ (the God who shows mercy).
17: εἰς αὐτὸ τοῦτο ἐξήγειρά σε (for this very purpose I raised you up) — exēgeira means to raise up or bring onto the stage of history, not resurrection; the LXX uses it of raising up a king or leader. The divine use of Pharaoh's resistance serves two stated purposes: display of divine power and proclamation of the divine name in all the earth — global redemptive-historical purposes, not merely punitive ones.
18: ὃν θέλει σκληρύνει (he hardens whom he wills) — the assertion is unqualified here. The Exodus narrative shows a more complex process (Pharaoh first hardens himself; divine hardening follows in response), but Paul states the divine sovereignty side without qualification, letting the objection it raises drive the argument of vv. 19–21 forward. Sklērynō is the LXX Exodus term.
19: τί ἔτι μέμφεται (why does he still find fault) — memphomai = to censure; the questioner treats divine sovereignty as eliminating moral responsibility. τῷ βουλήματι αὐτοῦ (his will/purpose) — boulēma is the deliberate, determined resolve, stronger than mere inclination (thelēma).
20: ὦ ἄνθρωπε — the direct vocative intensifies the rebuke. μενοῦνγε (nay, rather!) — a strong adversative particle. Paul's response is not a philosophical solution to the problem of sovereignty but an appeal to creaturely limits: the formed thing does not interrogate the former. The potter-clay image from Isa 29:16 and Jer 18:6 frames what follows.
21: ἐξουσίαν (authority/right) — the potter's sovereign authority over the clay. The same word is used of Jesus's authority throughout the Gospels. The language of honor and dishonor (εἰς τιμήν / εἰς ἀτιμίαν) belongs to the Roman household familiar to Paul's audience — vessels for special use versus vessels for common use.
22: The sentence is grammatically incomplete — an anacoluthon: "What if God, willing to show wrath and make power known, bore with great patience the objects of wrath prepared for destruction..." The main clause never arrives; the reader must supply the conclusion. κατηρτισμένα (perfect passive participle, "prepared for destruction") — the passive voice leaves the agent ambiguous. The middle reading ("who fitted themselves") has defenders; the passive reading (God as agent) is more natural but is not resolved, and Paul does not resolve it.
23: πλοῦτος τῆς δόξης αὐτοῦ (the riches of his glory) — the purpose clause identifies the positive side: vessels of mercy demonstrate the wealth of divine glory. Proētoimasen (prepared in advance) uses the same root as katērtismena in v. 22 but with a different preposition and direction; the advance preparation for glory frames the mercy.
24: οὓς καὶ ἐκάλεσεν (whom he also called) — these vessels of mercy are identified as drawn from both Jews and Gentiles. The preposition ἐξ (from/out of) in both phrases indicates that the called community is drawn from within the larger ethnic communities, not identified with them entirely.
25: Hosea 2:23 is applied to Gentiles: καλέσω τὸν οὐ λαόν μου λαόν μου (I will call the not-my-people my people). The original Hosea passage addresses apostate Israel, not Gentiles; Paul applies it to the broader principle that God calls into covenant status those who had none. The reversal of "not-my-people" to "my people" is accomplished by the divine speech act of calling, not by prior merit.
26: Hosea 1:10 continues: where the designation "not my people" was spoken, there the name "sons of the living God" will be given. The locative force (ἐκεῖ, there) marks a spatial coincidence between shame and restoration — the very site of rejection becomes the site of naming.
27: Isaiah 10:22–23 (LXX) introduces the remnant motif: though Israel's number is like the sand of the sea, only τὸ ὑπόλειμμα (the remnant) will be saved. This is not comfort for small numbers but a demonstration that within the large physical community, a smaller elect community is the operative concept — the same logic as the Isaac/Jacob arguments.
28: λόγον γὰρ συντελῶν καὶ συντέμνων (the Lord will accomplish his word, completing and cutting short) — the double participle emphasizes both completion and compression of the divine work; judgment is swift and decisive, not extended or partial.
29: Isaiah 1:9 is introduced as spoken by Isaiah προείρηκεν (has said beforehand) — the perfect tense gives the OT word ongoing relevance. κύριος σαβαώθ — Paul follows the LXX transliteration of YHWH Tzevaʾot (Lord of armies/hosts). The point is survival of a remnant by divine preservation alone; without that intervention, complete destruction would have followed.
30: The conclusion of the chapter's argument is stated as a paradox: Gentiles who did not pursue righteousness have attained it (κατέλαβεν, apprehended), specifically δικαιοσύνην δὲ τὴν ἐκ πίστεως (a righteousness from faith). Pursuit is not the mechanism — attainment comes by an unexpected route.
31: Israel, by contrast, pursued νόμον δικαιοσύνης (a law of righteousness) and did not arrive at the law. The failure is not in the goal (righteousness is the right goal) but in the mode: they pursued ὡς ἐξ ἔργων (as if from works) rather than receiving it from God through faith.
32: προσέκοψαν τῷ λίθῳ τοῦ προσκόμματος — they stumbled over the stone of stumbling. The composite quotation in v. 33 combines Isa 8:14 (a stone of stumbling and rock of offense) with Isa 28:16 (the precious cornerstone in Zion). Both Isaiah passages announce a divine purpose that confounds those who refuse to trust what God is doing.
33: The two Isaiah texts are woven together: ἰδοὺ τίθημι ἐν Σιὼν λίθον προσκόμματος from Isa 8:14, and ὁ πιστεύων ἐπ' αὐτῷ οὐ καταισχυνθήσεται (the one who believes on him will not be put to shame) from Isa 28:16. The conflation is an early Christian exegetical practice (cf. 1 Pet 2:6–8). Paul uses both texts as pointing to Christ: simultaneously cornerstone for those who believe and stumbling stone for those who do not.
